caseStudies.description:
I spent three years at SimilarWeb working across Core Platform Products — the navigation redesign, the Website Segments builder, and two new pods launched from scratch (Market Research and Demand Analysis). Across all of them, I worked closely with PMs and key stakeholders to shape product strategy and define design 
direction.

Alongside this, I pushed for system-level consistency, championed UX, set clearer cross-designer process, ran Figma sessions for engineers, and hired and mentored designers across teams.
caseStudies.scope: Product Strategy, Information Architecture,  Stakeholder Alignment, 0⇢1 Product Launch

sections.title: Sidebar redesign
sections.description:
Six months after the previous redesign, the navigation was still broken: unaligned IA across product sections, users losing filters, inputs and context between pages, inconsistent visual hierarchy and patterns.

Bottom-up initiative. Started as a 1.5-hour design exercise, became a cross-team push we sold to stakeholders ourselves. I led discovery through delivery across 5 designers, 2 team leads, PMs, FE/BE, and UX writer.

The Acquisition homepage was the structural blocker. Its owners defended it until a 0.6% engagement number settled the argument. Removing it unlocked simplified IA across the entire platform.

**Nav time 1.3 min → 42 sec (-53%); MAU increased by 2-3X on buried pages; Median pageviews per session 4.5 → 5 (+10%).**

sections.title: Website Segments
sections.description:
Website Segments lets users group URLs by specific parameters to track any defined slices of sites separately. ~35% of platform revenue comes from this functionality, with pricing scaled by segment quota — even Top Tier Enterprise packages capped at 25 segments, so every new segment was direct revenue. 

Segment builder completion rate was stuck at 64% and CS managers were doing most of the creation for clients. In close collaboration with VP of Product and PM we defined three concrete blockers: opaque Include/Exclude logic, a multi-step wizard misaligned with users' mental models, and no final URL list preview. 

I owned the full redesign cycle from discovery to delivery. Single-screen builder, live URL list preview, two clear Include/Exclude buckets editable side-by-side. 

**Completion rate 64% → 81% (+16.8pp); Time to create 4 min 5 sec → 23 sec (-90.6%); Segments per user +0.633.**

caseStudies.description: Sber spun up an internal PFM (Personal Financial Management) startup to address Russia's savings and financial education problem — 43% with no savings, 82% not planning expenses, 98% not investing. The Tribe began with five product teams and grew to twelve over three years. I owned design for the Total Funds product group, which started as one team and grew into three during my tenure.
caseStudies.scope: Product Strategy, Design Thinking, Information Architecture, Stakeholder Alignment

heroImage.alt: Total Funds redesign — old 12-segment product pie versus new asset-type donut with leading buttons

sections.title: Total Funds rebuild
sections.description: I owned design for Total Funds with a PM and two other designers. The old version was a 12-segment product pie nobody read as 'composition of capital'. We replaced it with a donut grouped by asset type (Cards, Savings, Investments, outside-Sber) plus leading buttons for next-product actions. The donut isn't the most accurate chart and adjacent teams pushed back — we held the line and partnered with Sber's gamification team on the spin: haptics, rolling numbers, color logic per category. ~88% of users actually spun it. Total Funds became Sber's 2nd-most-popular entry to the inner market — ~13% of customers came through, generating $20M+ in invest-product cross-sell in 2021.

images.alt: Total Funds widget inside the main Sber app surface
Five filter states of the asset donut: All, Cards, Savings, Investments, Outside Sber

sections.title: Financial Cushion
sections.description: Inside Total Funds, the standard 'set a goal' flow had weak follow-through — people set targets, then dropped off. We built a dedicated Financial Cushion flow framed in human terms ('for what?', 'how much do I need?'), auto-calculating the amount from the user's own monthly spending. I designed the flow with the team and helped scope the cut of a much-loved sibling idea — 'Business Angel,' a feature letting a trusted third party hold part of your wallet, scrapped because the fiduciary structure raised juridical issues we couldn't resolve. Users who set up a cushion from Total Funds continued accumulating at +81% the rate of users with ordinary Targets.

images.alt: Financial Cushion setup flow with auto-calculated target

sections.title: We were wrong
sections.description: Post-launch metrics inverted our assumption. In user testing people understood the deep-analysis tools — filters, history slicing, multi-angle grouping — but in production, a funny-small percentage actually used them. Even our high-income target users wanted simpler triggers, not analytical surface area. We ran another round of research focused on real usage; I owned the cut list with the PM. Final reduced foreground: balance, open-a-product, switch-grouping. The rest pushed into secondary navigation or out entirely. Retention climbed to 5.3% (from 2.6%), conversion to inner-market sales kept rising, CSI lifted. Testing measures comprehension; production measures want.

images.alt: Stripped-back Total Funds view with capital history graph
Dark mode treatment of the simplified Total Funds view

caseStudies.description: We built Control Tower because no existing tool gave us what we actually needed — a single place to see how the studio is doing. Not just project status, but the full picture: who's stretched, what's profitable, where there's room to take on more. It's an internal tool, built for how we work, pulling everything into one AI-native surface so decisions about capacity, hiring, and client work happen based on real numbers instead of gut feel.
caseStudies.scope: UI/UX Design, Design System, Product Strategy, Team Leadership
